bibliographic_data.title: Pb-Hash: Partitioned b-bit Hashing
bibliographic_data.abstract: Many hashing algorithms including minwise hashing (MinHash), one permutation hashing (OPH), and consistent weighted sampling (CWS) generate integers of B bits. With k hashes for each data vector, the storage would be B × k bits; and when used for large-scale learning, the model size would be 2B × k, which can be expensive. A standard strategy is to use only the lowest b bits out of the B bits and somewhat increase k, the number of hashes. In this study, we propose to re-use the hashes by partitioning the B bits into m chunks, e.g., b × m = B. Correspondingly, the model size becomes m × 2b × k, which can be substantially smaller than the original 2B × k.

core_content.novelty_claim: Proposes partitioned b-bit hashing (Pb-Hash) to re-use hashes by dividing B bits into m chunks, reducing the model size while maintaining accuracy.
core_content.key_methodology: Partitioning hash values into m chunks of B/m bits and using embedding tables for each hash.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Many hashing algorithms, such as minwise hashing (MinHash), generate integers of B bits. Using k hashes per data vector results in a large storage requirement of B × k bits for machine learning models. To reduce this, the common approach is to use only the lowest b bits out of B bits and increase the number of hashes k. This paper proposes partitioned b-bit hashing (Pb-Hash), where B bits are divided into m chunks, each with b bits, reducing the model size to m × 2b × k. Theoretical analysis shows that accuracy drops when using fewer than 4 chunks but still works well for larger values of m. Pb-Hash is evaluated in machine learning tasks, including linear SVM models and deep learning models, with an empirical evaluation on four pooling schemes: concatenation, max pooling, mean pooling, and product pooling.
